title: Dialogue and quotes drill
description: Pure dialogue passage — train quote+Shift+punctuation under speed
text: "What time is the meeting?" Sarah asked. "At three," David replied, "in the main conference room." She nodded. "Should I bring the report?" "Yes," he said, "and the projections too. They'll want to see both."

tips.text: Quotation marks run through fiction, interviews, and half the messages you send. They are worth getting right.
Open a quote and your hands should already know to close it. Let that pairing become reflex, not a decision.

tips.target: "What time is the meeting?" Sarah asked. "At three," David replied, "in the main conference room." She

tips.text: The little tags, he said and she asked, are the quiet hinges of a scene. Type them without a pause and the conversation keeps its pace.

tips.target: nodded. "Should I bring the report?" "Yes," he said, "and the projections too. They'll want to see both."

character_tips.anna: Dialogue is the trickiest punctuation there is. Master it and the rest of writing feels easy. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.maxim: A good deal of business writing is one person quoting another. Drill these marks until they are automatic. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.knopych: Two voices, back and forth. The marks keep them apart, so set them down cleanly and stay in step. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
character_tips.klavochka: A conversation set on the page. Get the quotes right and the reader hears every word. - Eleanor Hayes, editor
